enrichment.short_description: PrivyDrop is a free, open-source, peer-to-peer file and clipboard sharing web app that moves files directly between devices using WebRTC, with no account, upload limit, or server storage.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source (MIT license); no paid tiers
enrichment.seo_title: PrivyDrop Review 2026: Free P2P File and Clipboard Sharing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PrivyDrop is a free, open-source WebRTC tool for encrypted peer-to-peer file and clipboard sharing with no signup, size limits, or server storage. See features and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: PrivyDrop is a free, open-source, browser-based file and clipboard sharing tool built on WebRTC peer-to-peer technology. It positions itself as a privacy-first alternative to AirDrop that works across any modern browser and operating system rather than being locked to a single device ecosystem. The core idea is that files, folders, images, and rich text never pass through or get stored on a central server; instead, data moves directly between the sender and receiver's devices over an encrypted peer-to-peer connection established via a shared link or scanned QR code. Because there is no server-side relay for the actual file content, PrivyDrop advertises unlimited file size and transfer speed limited only by the users' own network connection, along with support for resumable transfers if a connection drops mid-transfer and the ability to send entire folders rather than single files at a time. The project also includes a clipboard-sharing mode that lets users send and receive formatted rich text instantly between devices, which is useful for quickly moving notes, code snippets, or links across a phone and a laptop without email or a chat app. PrivyDrop is developed and maintained by an independent developer using the GitHub handle david-bai00, who has described building the project partly as a personal solution for frictionless file transfer between their own devices and partly as a hands-on learning exercise in full-stack development. The source code is hosted publicly on GitHub under the MIT license, meaning anyone can inspect, self-host, fork, or modify the application; the stack is built with Next.js, React, Node.js, Express, WebRTC, and Socket.IO, and the project supports Docker-based deployment for teams or individuals who want to run their own instance instead of using the hosted version at privydrop.app. Because the project is free, open source, and run by an individual developer rather than a registered company, there is no publicly disclosed founding date, headquarters, funding history, or company size in the traditional sense - it functions more like a community open-source utility than a commercial SaaS vendor. There is no pricing page, subscription plan, or paid tier; the hosted web app at privydrop.app is entirely free to use, with no registration, file-size cap, or watermarking. The interface supports multiple languages including English, Chinese, Spanish, Japanese, German, French, and Korean, reflecting an international user base drawn largely from privacy-conscious individuals, freelancers, and small teams who need a quick, no-install way to move files between a desktop and a phone or between two people without relying on cloud storage intermediaries like Google Drive or WeTransfer. Because it has no company backing, users evaluating it for business-critical or compliance-sensitive workflows should weigh the lack of formal support, SLAs, or vendor accountability against its strong technical privacy properties.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PrivyDrop is a free, open-source web application for sharing files, folders, and clipboard content directly between devices using WebRTC peer-to-peer transfer. Rather than uploading a file to a server and generating a download link the way many file-sharing tools do, PrivyDrop establishes a direct encrypted connection between the sender's and receiver's browsers, so the file content itself never touches a third-party server.

The project is built and maintained by an independent developer and distributed under the MIT open-source license, with the full source code available on GitHub. It can be used as a hosted web app at privydrop.app or self-hosted via Docker for teams that want complete control over their own deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: PrivyDrop's core feature set centers on privacy and simplicity: end-to-end encrypted peer-to-peer transfer with no server-side storage of file content, no account creation, and no advertised limit on file size beyond what the users' network connection can support.

Beyond basic file transfer, PrivyDrop supports whole-folder transfers, resumable uploads if a connection is interrupted, rich-text clipboard sharing for quickly moving formatted notes between devices, and link- or QR-code-based pairing so a desktop and a phone can connect in seconds without typing an address.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: PrivyDrop has no pricing plans. The hosted version at privydrop.app is completely free to use, with no premium tier, storage quota, or watermarking.

Because the code is open source under the MIT license, organizations that need more control can self-host PrivyDrop on their own infrastructure at no licensing cost, though they would take on their own hosting and maintenance overhead.

features.title: Peer-to-peer WebRTC transfer
features.description: Files move directly between sender and receiver devices over an encrypted WebRTC connection rather than being uploaded to a central server.

features.title: No account required
features.description: Users can send and receive files immediately via a shared link or QR code, without signing up or logging in.

features.title: Unlimited file size
features.description: There is no advertised cap on individual file size; transfer speed depends on the users' own network connection.

features.title: Folder transfers
features.description: Entire folders can be sent in one transfer rather than selecting files individually.

features.title: Resumable transfers
features.description: Interrupted transfers can resume rather than restarting from the beginning.

features.title: Rich-text clipboard sharing
features.description: Users can send and receive formatted text instantly between devices, useful for notes, code, or links.

features.title: QR code and link pairing
features.description: Devices connect by scanning a QR code or opening a shared link, making desktop-to-mobile transfers fast.

features.title: Open-source and self-hostable
features.description: The MIT-licensed source code is on GitHub and can be self-hosted via Docker for full control over the deployment.

pros: Completely free with no paid tier or feature paywall
No account creation or personal data collection required to use it
File content is never stored on a central server, reducing data-exposure risk
Open-source code can be independently audited or self-hosted
No advertised file-size limit
cons: No company backing means no formal support channels or SLAs
Founding date, team size, and roadmap are not publicly disclosed
Both devices generally need to be online simultaneously since transfer is peer-to-peer
Lacks enterprise features like admin controls, audit logs, or centralized user management
Relies on browser WebRTC support, which can behave inconsistently on some networks or firewalls

faqs.question: Is PrivyDrop really free?
faqs.answer: Yes. PrivyDrop has no paid plans; the hosted web app is free to use with no file-size cap or watermarking.

faqs.question: Does PrivyDrop store my files on a server?
faqs.answer: No. Files transfer directly between devices over an encrypted peer-to-peer WebRTC connection and are not stored on PrivyDrop's servers.

faqs.question: Do I need to create an account to use PrivyDrop?
faqs.answer: No account or login is required. You simply open the site, share a link or QR code, and transfer files.

faqs.question: Can I self-host PrivyDrop?
faqs.answer: Yes. The source code is open source under the MIT license and available on GitHub, with Docker support for self-hosting.

faqs.question: Is there a file size limit?
faqs.answer: PrivyDrop does not advertise a fixed file-size limit; practical limits depend on the devices and network connection involved.

faqs.question: What happens if my transfer is interrupted?
faqs.answer: PrivyDrop supports resumable transfers, so an interrupted transfer can continue rather than starting over.

faqs.question: Who built PrivyDrop?
faqs.answer: PrivyDrop is developed and maintained by an independent open-source developer rather than a registered company.

faqs.question: What languages does PrivyDrop support?
faqs.answer: The interface is available in English, Chinese, Spanish, Japanese, German, French, and Korean.

use_cases.title: Quick device-to-device transfer
use_cases.description: Sending photos, documents, or folders between a phone and a laptop without cables or cloud storage.

use_cases.title: Privacy-sensitive file sharing
use_cases.description: Sharing sensitive documents without uploading them to a third-party cloud storage provider.

use_cases.title: Ad-hoc collaboration
use_cases.description: Quickly sharing a file or clipboard snippet with a colleague or client during a call without account setup.

enrichment.short_description: Process Street is a no-code workflow and checklist platform that helps teams document, run, and track recurring processes, approvals, and compliance operations.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered subscription (Startup, Pro, Enterprise), billed monthly or annually, with custom quotes for Pro and Enterprise
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, United States (with additional offices in London and Sydney)
enrichment.seo_title: Process Street Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Process Street is a no-code workflow and checklist platform for recurring processes and compliance. Compare Process Street pricing, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Process Street is a no-code workflow, checklist, and compliance operations platform founded in 2014 and headquartered in San Francisco, California, with additional offices in London and Sydney. The company built its product around the idea of giving operations, HR, IT, and compliance teams a 'GitHub for knowledge workers' style tool: a way to turn recurring business processes such as employee onboarding, client onboarding, vendor approvals, incident response, and audit checklists into structured, trackable workflows rather than static documents or spreadsheets. Users build workflows from customizable templates using a document-like editor, add conditional logic so steps branch based on answers, attach forms and file uploads to individual tasks, and assign steps to specific team members with due dates. Process Street reports metrics through an analytics dashboard, and workflows can trigger automations through native integrations and API access, plus connections to tools such as Slack and Microsoft Teams. Process Street raised a $12 million Series A led by Accel in February 2020 to expand its no-code workflow platform, following earlier seed funding, and has continued to position itself increasingly around compliance and operations use cases such as SOC 2 and ISO audit readiness in addition to general business process management. As of 2026 the company is reported to have roughly 55 to 62 employees, making it a small, focused SaaS vendor rather than a large enterprise software company. Process Street's pricing follows a three-tier subscription model: a Startup plan aimed at small businesses (fewer than 15 employees and under $2 million in revenue) priced at roughly $100 per month or $1,000 per year for five paid team members plus ten guest users, up to 5,000 data set records, unlimited file storage with a 5MB per-file cap, ten automation apps, and 100 automation actions per month; a Pro plan, the most popular tier, starting around $415 per month or $5,000 per year with expanded user limits, 10,000 data set records, 100MB per-file storage limit, all automation apps, advanced customization, business intelligence integrations, and a dedicated customer success manager; and an Enterprise plan starting around $1,660 per month or $20,000 per year with custom user and data limits, unlimited API calls, fully managed workflows, custom integrations, and personalized training and onboarding. Process Street also offers discounted Pro-plan pricing for small businesses meeting the Startup-plan criteria, along with discounts for registered non-profits, a 14-day free trial with no credit card required on the Pro plan, and the ability to purchase through the AWS Marketplace. The product is used primarily by operations, HR, and compliance teams inside mid-sized businesses that need repeatable, auditable processes but do not want to build custom internal tooling or rely on static checklists in documents and spreadsheets. Its main competitors in the workflow and business process management space include tools like Trainual, Tallyfy, Kissflow, and general-purpose work management platforms such as monday.com and Asana when used for checklist-style processes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Process Street is a no-code workflow and checklist platform, founded in 2014 and based in San Francisco, that helps teams turn recurring business processes into structured, trackable workflows. It is commonly used for employee and client onboarding, approvals, audits, and other repeatable operations where consistency and accountability matter.

The company raised a $12 million Series A round led by Accel in 2020 to expand its no-code workflow builder, and has since leaned further into compliance operations use cases such as audit readiness alongside its core business process management functionality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Process Street workflows are built from a document-style editor that supports conditional logic, embedded forms, file uploads, task assignment, due dates, and approval steps, so a single workflow can branch and route based on how earlier steps were answered.

The platform includes an analytics dashboard for tracking workflow completion and bottlenecks, native automation apps and actions for connecting to other tools, API access for custom integrations, and direct integrations with collaboration tools like Slack and Microsoft Teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Process Street offers three tiers: Startup (around $100/month or $1,000/year) for small businesses, Pro (around $415/month or $5,000/year) as the most popular plan for growing teams, and Enterprise (around $1,660/month or $20,000/year) for large organizations needing custom limits and managed workflows.
Discounted Pro-plan pricing is available for qualifying small businesses and registered non-profits, and a 14-day free trial requiring no credit card is offered on the Pro plan.

features.description: Track workflow completion rates, bottlenecks, and process performance over time.

features.title: Automations and integrations
features.description: Connect workflows to other business tools via automation apps, actions, and API access.

features.title: Slack and Microsoft Teams integration
features.description: Trigger and receive workflow notifications directly inside existing collaboration tools.

features.title: Compliance operations support
features.description: Templates and structures geared toward audit readiness and regulatory compliance tracking.

pros: Purpose-built for turning recurring processes into trackable, repeatable workflows
Conditional logic allows complex, branching processes without custom development
Discounted pricing available for qualifying small businesses and non-profits
Strong fit for compliance and audit-readiness use cases
14-day free trial with no credit card required
cons: Exact pricing for Pro and Enterprise plans requires contacting sales
Smaller company size (roughly 55-62 employees) compared to larger workflow platform vendors
Startup plan's per-file storage cap (5MB) is restrictive for larger attachments
Steeper cost jump between Startup and Pro tiers for growing teams

faqs.question: What is Process Street used for?
faqs.answer: Process Street is used to document and run recurring business processes such as onboarding, approvals, audits, and compliance checklists as structured, trackable workflows.

faqs.question: How much does Process Street cost?
faqs.answer: Process Street's Startup plan starts around $100/month, Pro around $415/month, and Enterprise around $1,660/month, all with annual billing discounts and custom quotes available.

faqs.question: Does Process Street offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Process Street offers a 14-day free trial on the Pro plan with no credit card required.

faqs.question: Is there a discount for small businesses or non-profits?
faqs.answer: Yes, Process Street offers reduced Pro-plan pricing for businesses under 15 employees and $2 million in revenue, as well as discounts for registered non-profits.

faqs.question: Where is Process Street headquartered?
faqs.answer: Process Street is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with additional offices in London and Sydney.

faqs.question: When was Process Street founded?
faqs.answer: Process Street was founded in 2014.

faqs.question: Does Process Street support integrations?
faqs.answer: Yes, Process Street offers native automation apps and actions, API access, and integrations with tools like Slack and Microsoft Teams.

faqs.question: Can Process Street handle approval workflows?
faqs.answer: Yes, workflows can include approval steps, conditional logic, and task assignment with due dates.

enrichment.short_description: ProcessWire is a free, open-source content management system and PHP framework created by developer Ryan Cramer, offering a flexible API-first architecture for building custom websites.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source (MPL 2.0 license); optional paid Pro modules and hosting-related add-ons
enrichment.company_size: Small core team led by creator Ryan Cramer, supported by an open-source community
enrichment.seo_title: ProcessWire Review 2026: Open-Source CMS and PHP Framework
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ProcessWire is a free, open-source CMS and PHP framework built by Ryan Cramer for developers who want a flexible, API-first content management system.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ProcessWire is a free and open-source content management system and PHP framework created by developer Ryan Cramer. Its roots trace back to 2003, when Cramer, who studied computer science and media arts and design, started his own web development company, Ryan Cramer Design, LLC, and began building an internal tool originally called Dictator CMS to solve recurring problems he encountered across client projects. Cramer used and refined this internal tool privately for about seven years before renaming it ProcessWire and publicly releasing it as open-source software in 2010 under the Mozilla Public License 2.0, which permits free use, modification, and distribution while requiring that changes to the licensed code itself be shared under the same license. The motivation for open-sourcing it was to expand ProcessWire's reach beyond the roughly dozen sites Cramer personally worked on each year and to let a wider community of developers contribute to and benefit from the project. Unlike many mainstream CMS platforms that prioritize non-technical end users, ProcessWire is explicitly developer-oriented: it exposes a clean, well-documented PHP API for querying and manipulating content, uses a flexible fields-and-templates data model rather than rigid content types, and includes built-in features such as role-based access control, native multi-language content support, and a modular plugin architecture with an extensive module directory maintained by the community. Because it functions as much like a PHP framework as a traditional CMS, ProcessWire is popular among freelance developers and agencies building custom, non-templated websites where full control over markup and data structure matters more than pre-built themes. As of estimates cited around 2021, ProcessWire was used by more than 5,000 web developers worldwide with an estimated 100,000-plus installations, and the project continues to be actively maintained. ProcessWire is not a venture-backed startup or a company with disclosed headcount, funding, or revenue figures in the way SaaS products are; it is maintained primarily by Ryan Cramer through his company, supported by a community of module developers, forum contributors, and third-party service providers who build ProcessWire-based sites for clients. There is no subscription fee for the core CMS or framework itself, which can be downloaded and self-hosted on any standard PHP and MySQL/MariaDB hosting environment. Cramer offers a small number of optional paid 'Pro' modules (sold separately through the ProcessWire site) that add advanced functionality such as enhanced fields, forms, or repeaters, but these are optional add-ons rather than a requirement to use the platform. Because ProcessWire is self-hosted, total cost of ownership depends on hosting, developer time, and any Pro modules purchased rather than a recurring platform fee. It is frequently compared to other open-source, developer-focused CMS platforms such as Craft CMS, Kirby, and WordPress (when used headless or with a code-first approach), and is distinguished by its lightweight core, API flexibility, and emphasis on giving developers full control rather than imposing a specific front-end templating convention.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ProcessWire is a free, open-source content management system and PHP framework created by developer Ryan Cramer. It began as an internal tool at his web development company in 2003 and was publicly released as open-source software under the Mozilla Public License 2.0 in 2010.

Unlike consumer-facing CMS platforms, ProcessWire is built for developers: it exposes a clean PHP API, a flexible fields-and-templates content model, and a modular architecture, making it well suited to custom-built, non-templated websites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ProcessWire's core strengths include a flexible fields-and-templates data model rather than fixed content types, a well-documented PHP API for querying and manipulating content programmatically, and role-based access control for managing editor and administrator permissions.

The platform also includes native multi-language content support out of the box, a modular plugin architecture backed by an extensive community module directory, and a lightweight core that keeps sites fast without requiring heavy frameworks on top.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The ProcessWire core CMS and framework are entirely free to download and self-host, with no subscription fee, under the Mozilla Public License 2.0.

Ryan Cramer offers a small set of optional paid Pro modules that extend functionality, such as enhanced fields, forms, or repeaters, but these are optional purchases rather than a requirement for running the platform.

features.title: Flexible fields-and-templates model
features.description: Content structure is defined through customizable fields and templates rather than fixed content types, giving developers full control over data structure.

features.title: PHP API-first design
features.description: A well-documented PHP API lets developers query, manipulate, and render content programmatically without being locked into a rigid theming system.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: Granular permissions let administrators control what different user roles can view or edit within the admin interface.

features.title: Native multi-language support
features.description: Multi-language content and admin interface support is built into the core rather than requiring a third-party plugin.

features.title: Modular plugin architecture
features.description: An extensive community-maintained module directory allows functionality to be extended without modifying core code.

features.title: Lightweight, self-hosted core
features.description: The core system is lightweight and runs on standard PHP and MySQL/MariaDB hosting, keeping sites fast and portable.

features.title: Optional Pro modules
features.description: Paid Pro modules from the creator add advanced functionality like enhanced fields, forms, and repeaters for developers who need them.

features.title: Active developer community
features.description: An established forum and module directory support developers building and maintaining ProcessWire sites.

pros: Completely free and open source with no recurring platform fee
Highly flexible, developer-friendly API well suited to custom-built websites
Native multi-language support built into the core
Lightweight core keeps sites fast and reduces bloat
Established, active developer community and module ecosystem
cons: Requires PHP development knowledge; not designed for non-technical end users
Smaller ecosystem and talent pool compared to mainstream CMS platforms like WordPress
No official company, support contracts, or SLAs behind the project
Self-hosting means users are responsible for their own hosting, backups, and security updates
Fewer pre-built themes or templates compared to more mainstream CMS platforms

faqs.question: Is ProcessWire free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core ProcessWire CMS and framework are free and open source under the Mozilla Public License 2.0. Optional paid Pro modules are available separately.

faqs.question: Who created ProcessWire?
faqs.answer: ProcessWire was created by developer Ryan Cramer, originally as an internal tool for his web development company starting in 2003, before being publicly released as open source in 2010.

faqs.question: Is ProcessWire suitable for non-developers?
faqs.answer: ProcessWire is primarily designed for developers who want full control over site structure and code; it is less suited to non-technical users looking for a drag-and-drop website builder.

faqs.question: What license does ProcessWire use?
faqs.answer: ProcessWire is distributed under the Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPL 2.0).

faqs.question: Does ProcessWire support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, native multi-language content and admin interface support is built into the ProcessWire core.

faqs.question: What hosting does ProcessWire require?
faqs.answer: ProcessWire runs on standard PHP hosting with a MySQL or MariaDB database, similar to most self-hosted CMS platforms.

faqs.question: How many sites use ProcessWire?
faqs.answer: Estimates cited around 2021 put ProcessWire usage at more than 5,000 developers worldwide with over 100,000 installations.

faqs.question: Are there paid add-ons for ProcessWire?
faqs.answer: Yes, the creator offers optional paid Pro modules that add advanced functionality, though the core CMS remains free.

use_cases.title: Custom-built business websites

use_cases.audience: Web development agencies and freelance developers

use_cases.description: Organizations needing native multi-language content management without relying on a heavy third-party plugin.

features: Full open-source CMS and PHP framework

enrichment.short_description: Procore is a cloud-based construction management platform connecting owners, general contractors, and specialty contractors across project management, financials, quality and safety, and preconstruction.
enrichment.pricing_model: Custom annual quote based on Annual Construction Volume (ACV), with unlimited users included
languages: and additional languages (13 total supported in the web application)
enrichment.seo_title: Procore Review 2026: Construction Management Software Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Procore is a cloud-based construction management platform for project, financial, and quality and safety management. See Procore features, pricing model, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Procore Technologies is a publicly traded construction management software company founded in 2002 by Craig 'Tooey' Courtemanche, a former carpenter and real estate developer who built the first version of the product after realizing during the construction of his own home that technology could streamline the notoriously paper-heavy, fragmented workflows of the construction industry. The company is headquartered in Carpinteria, California, and trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker PCOR after completing its initial public offering on May 24, 2021, pricing shares at $67.00 and selling over 10.4 million shares. Procore has grown into one of the leading global providers of construction management software, connecting more than 2 million users across over 150 countries, and as of 2026 the company employs roughly 4,400 people, with sales and support the largest function at over 1,300 employees and engineering close behind at over 1,100. Tooey Courtemanche served as CEO and president from the company's founding in 2002 until November 2025, after which he transitioned to the role of Founder and Chairman of the Board. Procore's platform is built around connecting the many different parties involved in a construction project - owners, general contractors, and specialty/trade contractors - on a single system of record that spans the full project lifecycle. Its core product areas include project management (RFIs, submittals, drawings, daily logs, punch lists), financial management (budgeting, contracts, change orders, invoicing), quality and safety management (inspections, incident tracking, observations), preconstruction (estimating, bid management), and workforce/resource management. The platform is used heavily in the field via mobile apps as well as in the office, and it integrates with a broad ecosystem of third-party construction and accounting software through the Procore App Marketplace. Procore's pricing model is notably different from many SaaS products: rather than charging per user, Procore charges an upfront annual fee per product based on the customer's Annual Construction Volume (ACV) - the aggregate dollar value of construction work across the customer's projects - meaning larger contractors and owners with more construction volume pay more, but all users at a subscribing company can access the platform at no additional per-seat cost. This model is designed to encourage full adoption across a company's field and office staff without usage limits discouraging logins. Procore does not publish specific price points; prospective customers must contact Procore's sales team or call its published sales line to receive a customized estimate, and pricing typically involves an annual contract with options for multi-year commitments and volume protection. The web application supports numerous languages, including English, Spanish, French, German, Portuguese, and more recently Traditional Chinese and Italian in open beta, reflecting Procore's global customer base across commercial, residential, industrial, and infrastructure construction segments. Competitors in the construction management software space include Autodesk Construction Cloud, PlanGrid (now part of Autodesk), Buildertrend, and CoConstruct, among others.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Procore Technologies is a publicly traded (NYSE: PCOR) construction management software company founded in 2002 by Tooey Courtemanche and headquartered in Carpinteria, California. The platform connects owners, general contractors, and specialty contractors on a single system of record spanning project management, financials, quality and safety, and preconstruction.
Procore completed its IPO in May 2021 and has grown to serve more than 2 million users across over 150 countries, with roughly 4,400 employees as of 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Procore's core modules cover project management tools such as RFIs, submittals, drawing management, daily logs, and punch lists; financial management including budgeting, contracts, change orders, and invoicing; and quality and safety management for inspections, incident tracking, and safety observations.

The platform also supports preconstruction workflows like estimating and bid management, mobile field access for on-site teams, and a broad app marketplace of third-party integrations spanning accounting, scheduling, and design software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Procore uses a custom, quote-based pricing model rather than published per-user tiers. Customers pay an upfront annual fee per product, based on their Annual Construction Volume (ACV), with unlimited users, unlimited data storage, and 24/7 support included at no extra cost.
Because pricing is not publicly listed, prospective customers must contact Procore's sales team for a customized estimate; options include annual contracts, multi-year pools, and volume protection.

features.title: Project management
features.description: Manage RFIs, submittals, drawings, daily logs, and punch lists in one centralized system across office and field teams.

features.title: Financial management
features.description: Track budgets, contracts, change orders, and invoicing to keep project finances aligned with real-time progress.

features.title: Quality and safety management
features.description: Log inspections, safety observations, and incidents to support compliance and reduce jobsite risk.

features.title: Preconstruction tools
features.description: Estimating and bid management tools support project planning before construction begins.

features.title: Mobile field access
features.description: Field teams can access and update project data from mobile devices on the jobsite.

features.title: Unlimited users per contract
features.description: Pricing is based on construction volume rather than per-seat fees, so companies can add unlimited users.

features.title: App Marketplace integrations
features.description: Connects with a broad ecosystem of third-party construction and accounting software.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: The web application supports numerous languages, including English, Spanish, French, German, and Portuguese.

pros: Unlimited users included in pricing, encouraging full company-wide adoption
Comprehensive coverage across project management, financials, and safety in one platform
Large, established company with strong industry track record and global reach
Broad marketplace of integrations with other construction and accounting tools
Strong mobile field experience for on-site teams
cons: Pricing is not publicly disclosed and requires a sales conversation to obtain a quote
Pricing tied to Annual Construction Volume may be costly for very large contractors
Platform breadth can mean a learning curve for smaller teams that only need a subset of features
Annual contract model offers less flexibility than month-to-month SaaS pricing

faqs.question: How much does Procore cost?
faqs.answer: Procore does not publish fixed pricing. It charges an upfront annual fee per product based on the customer's Annual Construction Volume, and prospective customers must contact sales for a custom quote.

faqs.question: Does Procore charge per user?
faqs.answer: No. Procore's pricing model includes unlimited users at no additional per-seat cost; fees are based on Annual Construction Volume instead.

faqs.question: When was Procore founded?
faqs.answer: Procore was founded in 2002 by Tooey Courtemanche.

faqs.question: Is Procore a public company?
faqs.answer: Yes, Procore Technologies trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker PCOR after its May 2021 IPO.

faqs.question: Where is Procore headquartered?
faqs.answer: Procore is headquartered in Carpinteria, California.

faqs.question: What types of companies use Procore?
faqs.answer: Procore is used by owners, general contractors, and specialty/trade contractors across commercial, residential, industrial, and infrastructure construction.

faqs.question: Does Procore have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Procore offers mobile apps for field teams to access and update project data on the jobsite.

faqs.question: What languages does Procore support?
faqs.answer: Procore's web application supports numerous languages including English, Spanish, French, German, Portuguese, and more, with additional languages in ongoing rollout.

enrichment.short_description: Procurify is a cloud-based spend management platform that unifies purchasing, accounts payable, corporate cards, and contract management for mid-sized organizations.
enrichment.pricing_model: Custom quote-based pricing, modular add-ons, no per-user fee
enrichment.seo_title: Procurify Review 2026: Spend Management and Procurement Software
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Procurify is a cloud-based spend management platform for purchasing, accounts payable, and corporate cards. See Procurify pricing, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Procurify is a cloud-based spend management and procurement software company founded in 2012 by Kenneth Loi and Aman Mann and headquartered in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada. The company built its platform around the idea of giving mid-sized organizations end-to-end visibility and control over company spending, moving beyond narrow purchase-order software into a broader spend management suite. Procurify's core platform, sometimes referred to as the Procurify Platform, starts with purchasing functionality - requisitions, purchase orders, approval workflows, and vendor management - and can be extended with additional modules covering accounts payable automation (invoice capture, three-way matching, payment workflows), contract management, and expense and corporate card management, allowing organizations to consolidate multiple spend-related workflows that might otherwise be spread across separate point solutions. A key differentiator in Procurify's go-to-market is that it does not charge on a strict per-user basis the way many procurement platforms do, instead offering flexible pricing based on the features and support level a customer needs, which the company positions as more accommodating for organizations that want broad employee access to request purchases without paying a per-seat fee for every requester. Procurify has raised a total of approximately $82.5 million in funding across nine rounds from investors, and reported annual recurring revenue in the tens of millions of dollars in recent estimates. As of 2026, employee count estimates vary by source but generally fall in the range of roughly 150 to 180 people, positioning Procurify as a focused mid-market SaaS vendor rather than a large enterprise software company. The company's target customers tend to be mid-sized organizations across sectors like education, healthcare, non-profit, manufacturing, and technology that have outgrown manual, spreadsheet-based purchasing processes or basic purchase-order tools but do not need the full complexity (or cost) of large enterprise procure-to-pay suites such as Coupa or SAP Ariba. Procurify emphasizes ease of implementation, stating that its platform does not require dedicated IT expertise to set up, and it charges a one-time implementation fee that varies based on a customer's specific use cases and required integrations. Because Procurify does not publish specific pricing tiers or dollar figures on its website, prospective customers must request a custom quote or book a demo through the company's sales process; typical evaluation involves a conversation about which modules (Purchasing, Accounts Payable, Contracts, Expense and Card) a given organization needs, since customers can start with the base platform and purchasing module and add other modules as needs grow. This modular approach is aimed at organizations that want to consolidate spend management into a single system of record while controlling costs by only paying for the functionality they actually use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Procurify is a cloud-based spend management platform founded in 2012 by Kenneth Loi and Aman Mann and headquartered in Vancouver, Canada. It helps mid-sized organizations centralize purchasing, accounts payable, contracts, and corporate card spend in a single system rather than managing them across disconnected tools and spreadsheets.
The company has raised approximately $82.5 million in funding across nine rounds and employs roughly 150 to 180 people as of 2026, positioning it as a focused mid-market spend management vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Procurify's base platform covers purchasing workflows including requisitions, purchase orders, approval routing, and vendor management, giving organizations visibility and control over spend requests before money leaves the company.

Additional modules extend the platform into accounts payable automation, contract management, and expense and corporate card management, allowing customers to consolidate multiple spend-related processes into one connected system as their needs grow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Procurify uses a custom, quote-based pricing model rather than publicly listed tiers, with flexible pricing based on the features and support level a customer needs rather than a strict per-user fee.

Customers typically start with the base Procurify Platform and Purchasing module, then add Accounts Payable, Contracts, or Expense and Card modules as needed; a one-time implementation fee applies based on use cases and required integrations.

cons: Pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation to obtain a quote
One-time implementation fee adds to upfront cost beyond the subscription
Smaller company size compared to large enterprise procure-to-pay vendors like Coupa or SAP Ariba
Full functionality depends on purchasing additional modules beyond the base platform

faqs.question: How much does Procurify cost?
faqs.answer: Procurify does not publish fixed pricing. It offers flexible pricing based on features and support needs, and prospective customers must request a custom quote.

faqs.question: Does Procurify charge per user?
faqs.answer: No, Procurify does not use a strict per-user pricing model, which is intended to support broader employee access to purchasing requests.

faqs.question: When was Procurify founded?
faqs.answer: Procurify was founded in 2012 by Kenneth Loi and Aman Mann.

faqs.question: Where is Procurify headquartered?
faqs.answer: Procurify is headquartered in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada.

faqs.answer: Procurify's core platform includes Purchasing, with optional add-on modules for Accounts Payable, Contracts, and Expense and Card management.

faqs.question: Is there an implementation fee?
faqs.answer: Yes, Procurify charges a one-time implementation fee based on a customer's use cases and required integrations.

faqs.question: What size of company is Procurify best suited for?
faqs.answer: Procurify is designed primarily for mid-sized organizations that have outgrown manual or spreadsheet-based purchasing but do not need a large enterprise procure-to-pay suite.

faqs.answer: Procurify has raised approximately $82.5 million in funding across nine rounds.

enrichment.short_description: ProdPad is a modular product management platform for building roadmaps, capturing and prioritizing ideas, and managing customer feedback, letting teams pay only for the modules they use.

enrichment.seo_title: ProdPad Review 2026: Modular Product Management Software Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ProdPad is a modular product management platform for roadmaps, ideas, and feedback. Compare ProdPad pricing plans, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ProdPad is a product management software platform founded in 2012 by Simon Cast and Janna Bastow and headquartered in Brighton, England. The company built ProdPad around the idea that product managers should be able to assemble a toolset from distinct but connected modules rather than being forced into a single monolithic product, and its platform is organized into three core areas: Roadmaps, for building and communicating visual, outcome-oriented product roadmaps; Ideas, for capturing, evaluating, and prioritizing product ideas using frameworks like RICE or custom scoring; and Feedback, for collecting and organizing customer feedback and requests and linking them back to ideas and roadmap items so that product decisions stay traceable to real customer input. ProdPad's positioning emphasizes 'lean' product management practices and helping teams avoid roadmaps that are just feature-delivery timelines, instead focusing on outcomes and strategy. As a small, independently operated company, ProdPad has a notably lean team; employee count estimates place the company in roughly the 11-50 employee range on LinkedIn, with some third-party data sources suggesting even smaller current headcounts, making it a boutique vendor compared to venture-backed competitors like Productboard or Aha!. ProdPad's pricing model is distinctively modular and per-editor rather than following a simple tiered structure: each of the three modules (Roadmaps, Ideas, Feedback) has its own Essentials and Advanced pricing level, with published rates around $30 per editor per month for Essentials tiers and $45 to $55 per editor per month for Advanced tiers of individual modules, and organizations pay only for the modules and the number of editors (people who create or manage content) they need, while unlimited 'Reviewer' or contributor accounts, who can add ideas and feedback but not manage the system, are included free. For example, a five-person product team using all three modules together might pay in the range of $500 to $600 per month depending on module tier selections. Annual billing provides roughly a 20 percent discount compared to monthly billing, payments are processed through Recurly, and ProdPad offers a free trial (typically at least seven days, sometimes longer for engaged prospects) with no credit card required. ProdPad Enterprise is a separate custom-priced tier for large organizations that require additional security, support, or contract terms, and is only available by contacting the company's sales team directly. Because of its modular structure, ProdPad tends to appeal to product teams that want to start small - for instance, adopting only the Roadmaps module - and expand into Ideas and Feedback modules over time as their product management practice matures, rather than committing upfront to an all-in-one platform. This differs from many competitors that bundle roadmapping, idea management, and feedback collection into a single flat per-seat price regardless of which capabilities a team actually uses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ProdPad is a modular product management platform founded in 2012 by Simon Cast and Janna Bastow and headquartered in Brighton, England. The platform is organized into three connected modules - Roadmaps, Ideas, and Feedback - that teams can adopt individually or together.

As a small, independently run company with roughly 11-50 employees, ProdPad positions itself as a focused, lean alternative to larger venture-backed product management platforms, emphasizing outcome-driven roadmapping over simple feature-delivery timelines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Roadmaps module lets teams build visual, strategy-led product roadmaps that communicate outcomes rather than just feature release dates, while the Ideas module supports capturing and prioritizing ideas using frameworks like RICE scoring.

The Feedback module centralizes customer feedback and requests, linking them to ideas and roadmap items so product decisions stay traceable to real customer input, and unlimited free 'Reviewer' accounts let stakeholders contribute without a paid seat.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ProdPad prices each module - Roadmaps, Ideas, and Feedback - separately at Essentials and Advanced tiers, with published per-editor rates starting around $24-30/month for Essentials and $45-55/month for Advanced, billed per editor rather than per total user.

Annual billing offers roughly a 20 percent discount versus monthly billing, unlimited free Reviewer accounts are included, and a custom-priced Enterprise tier is available for larger organizations via direct sales contact.

Unlimited free reviewer accounts support broad stakeholder participation without added cost

Small, independently run company with a focused product vision
Free trial with no credit card required
cons: Modular per-editor pricing can be harder to predict than a simple flat per-seat plan
Smaller company size than larger competitors like Productboard may mean fewer resources for rapid feature development
Costs can add up quickly for larger teams using all three modules
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation

faqs.question: How does ProdPad pricing work?
faqs.answer: ProdPad prices its Roadmaps, Ideas, and Feedback modules separately on a per-editor basis, with Essentials and Advanced tiers for each module; customers pay only for the modules and editor seats they need.

faqs.question: When was ProdPad founded?
faqs.answer: ProdPad was founded in 2012 by Simon Cast and Janna Bastow.

faqs.answer: ProdPad is headquartered in Brighton, England, United Kingdom.

faqs.question: Does ProdPad offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, ProdPad offers a free trial of at least seven days, with no credit card required to start.

faqs.question: What is a ProdPad 'Reviewer' account?
faqs.answer: Reviewer accounts are free, unlimited contributor accounts that let stakeholders add ideas and feedback without needing a paid editor seat.

faqs.answer: Yes, annual billing typically offers around a 20 percent discount compared to monthly billing.

faqs.answer: Yes, ProdPad Enterprise is a custom-priced tier for large organizations, available by contacting ProdPad's sales team.

enrichment.seo_title: Product Fruits Review 2026: No-Code User Onboarding Software Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Product Fruits is a no-code digital adoption platform for product tours, onboarding checklists, and tooltips. Compare Product Fruits pricing, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Product Fruits is a no-code digital adoption platform founded in 2019 and headquartered in Prague, Czech Republic. The company was founded by Ladislav Salom, and the product is built to let product managers, customer success teams, and marketers build in-app user onboarding experiences without writing code or requiring engineering resources. Its core capabilities include interactive product tours and walkthroughs that guide new users through key actions, onboarding checklists that track a user's progress through setup steps, contextual hints and tooltips that surface guidance at the moment a user needs it, an in-app help/support widget, a feedback collection module for gathering user sentiment and feature requests, and more recently AI-assisted features such as an AI copilot that can answer in-app user questions by pulling from a company's own content sources. The platform is aimed primarily at software-as-a-service companies that want to improve trial-to-paid conversion, reduce time-to-value for new users, and cut down on support ticket volume by proactively guiding users to self-serve rather than contacting support. Product Fruits' pricing is structured around monthly active users (MAU) rather than a flat per-seat model, since digital adoption tools are typically deployed to a company's entire end-user base rather than to internal team members. The company publishes four main tiers: a Starter plan priced at $96 per month (dropping to roughly $72 per month with annual billing, a 25 percent discount) intended for smaller teams getting started with tours, hints, and checklists; a Pro plan at $149 per month (or roughly $112 per month annually) that expands limits on tours, hints, checklists, and adds text-based Copilot conversations and more user segments; a Business plan, also listed at $149 per month standard pricing with expanded usage limits including unlimited tours, hints, and checklists, more text conversations, voice-based onboarding minutes, and more team seats, positioned for companies with more complex adoption programs; and a Custom plan for larger organizations that need custom monthly active user limits, dedicated security reviews, and formal SLA agreements. All published tiers are based on a baseline of 1,500 monthly active users, with pricing scaling up via a calculator as an organization's MAU grows beyond that baseline; annual billing consistently provides a 25 percent discount across all tiers, and there are no setup fees or per-view charges layered on top of the subscription price. Because Product Fruits is a comparatively young and privately held company founded in 2019, detailed public information about its total funding, exact employee headcount, or revenue is limited, and the company does not appear to have disclosed institutional venture funding rounds in the way some competitors in the digital adoption platform category, such as WalkMe, Pendo, or Appcues, have. It is generally positioned as a lower-cost, easier-to-implement alternative to larger enterprise digital adoption platforms, appealing particularly to small and mid-sized SaaS companies that want onboarding and in-app guidance capability without the implementation overhead or price tag of enterprise-grade tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Product Fruits is a no-code digital adoption platform founded in 2019 by Ladislav Salom and headquartered in Prague, Czech Republic. It helps SaaS companies build in-app product tours, onboarding checklists, tooltips, and feedback flows without requiring engineering resources.
The platform is aimed at improving trial-to-paid conversion, speeding up new-user time-to-value, and reducing support ticket volume through proactive, self-serve in-app guidance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include interactive product tours and walkthroughs, onboarding checklists that track user progress, contextual hints and tooltips, and an in-app help widget for surfacing support content directly inside the product.

Product Fruits also includes a feedback collection module for gathering user sentiment and requests, plus newer AI-assisted features like a Copilot that can answer in-app user questions using a company's own content sources.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Product Fruits prices its Starter, Pro, and Business plans starting at $96, $149, and $149 per month respectively (with roughly 25 percent savings on annual billing), based on a baseline of 1,500 monthly active users, with pricing scaling as MAU grows.
A Custom plan is available for larger organizations that need higher MAU limits, dedicated security reviews, and formal SLA agreements.

faqs.answer: Product Fruits is a no-code digital adoption platform for building in-app product tours, onboarding checklists, tooltips, help widgets, and feedback collection.

faqs.question: How much does Product Fruits cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start at $96/month for Starter, with Pro and Business plans at $149/month, all based on a 1,500 monthly active user baseline, with roughly 25% savings on annual billing.

faqs.answer: Product Fruits was founded in 2019.

faqs.answer: Product Fruits is headquartered in Prague, Czech Republic.

faqs.answer: No, Product Fruits is designed as a no-code platform so product, marketing, and customer success teams can build onboarding flows without engineering.

faqs.answer: Yes, Product Fruits includes an AI Copilot that can answer in-app user questions using a company's own content sources.

faqs.answer: Pricing is based on monthly active users (MAU) rather than internal team seats, since onboarding tools are typically shown to a company's full end-user base.

enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium, tiered per-maker subscription pricing, billed monthly or annually

enrichment.seo_title: Productboard Review 2026: Product Management Software Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Productboard is a customer-driven product management platform for feedback, prioritization, and roadmapping. Compare Productboard pricing plans, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Productboard is a product management platform founded in 2014 by Hubert Palan and Daniel Hejl, dual-headquartered between San Francisco, California, and Prague, Czech Republic, with additional offices in Vancouver, Dublin, London, and Brno alongside a distributed remote team across Europe. Hubert Palan, who grew up in Prague and later moved to the San Francisco Bay Area, serves as founder and CEO; he holds a computer science degree from Czech Technical University in Prague and an MBA in entrepreneurship from UC Berkeley. Productboard's core positioning is 'customer-driven product management': the platform is designed to help product teams systematically capture feedback and feature requests from customers, sales teams, and support tickets, score and prioritize that input against strategic objectives, and translate prioritized work into shareable roadmaps that align engineering, sales, and executive stakeholders. Its main functional areas include a centralized feedback inbox for aggregating input from multiple channels, an insights and scoring engine for prioritizing features against customer and business impact, visual roadmap building and sharing tools including public-facing product portals, and integrations with tools like Salesforce, Jira, Slack, and Zendesk. The company has raised approximately $261.7 million in total funding across seven rounds, most recently a $125 million Series D round in 2022 that valued the company at approximately $1.7 billion, making it one of the more highly valued companies in the product management software category alongside competitors like Aha!. Productboard's differentiator has increasingly become AI: the platform includes an AI feature branded 'Spark' that helps summarize feedback, generate insights, and support prioritization decisions using a credit-based usage system built into its pricing tiers. As of recent estimates, Productboard reported roughly $71.8 million in annual recurring revenue and employs somewhere between approximately 330 and 340 people. Productboard's pricing follows a freemium, per-'maker' model, where a 'maker' is a paid seat for someone actively creating or managing product plans, while unlimited free contributor and viewer-type access is available depending on the tier. The Free plan includes 50 AI credits per month, full access to the Spark AI features, unlimited roadmaps, up to 500 feedback notes, 25 contributors, and one teamspace and product portal, with no credit card required to start. The Plus plan costs $19 per maker per month when billed annually (or $25 per maker per month billed monthly) and adds increased AI credits, customer segmentation, feedback loop closing, and product usage integrations. The Business plan, Productboard's most commonly recommended tier, costs $59 per maker per month billed annually (minimum two makers) or $75 per maker per month billed monthly, and includes unlimited feedback notes, unlimited teamspaces, a shared skills library, two product portals, and enhanced customization. The Enterprise plan is custom priced with a minimum of five makers billed annually and includes 800 AI credits per maker per month, unlimited contributors, SAML SSO, SCIM provisioning, custom roles, Salesforce integration, and live onboarding support. Annual billing saves roughly 21 percent compared to monthly billing across paid tiers, a 14-day free trial is available on the Business plan, and additional AI credit top-ups can be purchased separately if a team exceeds its plan's monthly allotment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Productboard is a customer-driven product management platform founded in 2014 by Hubert Palan and Daniel Hejl, with dual headquarters in San Francisco and Prague. The platform helps product teams capture customer feedback, prioritize features against strategic goals, and build shareable roadmaps.
The company has raised approximately $261.7 million in total funding, reached a $1.7 billion valuation in its 2022 Series D round, and employs roughly 330-340 people as of recent estimates.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Productboard centralizes customer feedback from multiple channels into a single inbox, then uses a scoring and prioritization engine to help teams weigh features against customer impact and business objectives.

The platform includes visual roadmap building and sharing tools, including public-facing product portals, along with integrations to tools like Salesforce, Jira, Slack, and Zendesk, plus an AI feature called Spark for summarizing feedback and supporting prioritization decisions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Productboard offers a Free plan with core roadmapping and limited AI credits, a Plus plan from $19/maker/month (annual billing), a Business plan from $59/maker/month (annual billing, minimum two makers), and a custom-priced Enterprise plan with a minimum of five makers.

Annual billing saves roughly 21 percent versus monthly billing, a 14-day free trial is available on the Business plan, and additional AI credit top-ups can be purchased if a team exceeds its plan's monthly allotment.

features.description: Score and rank features against customer impact and strategic objectives to guide roadmap decisions.

features.description: Build and share timeline-based or now-next-later roadmaps tailored to different stakeholder audiences.

features.description: Share external-facing roadmaps and collect feedback directly from customers through a branded portal.

features.description: Connect with tools like Salesforce, Jira, Slack, and Zendesk to sync feedback and roadmap data.

features.description: Organize work into teamspaces with role-based access for different product teams.

Free plan available with real functionality, including limited AI features
Well-funded, established company with a large user base and strong integration ecosystem
AI-powered Spark features help speed up feedback analysis and prioritization
Flexible per-maker pricing that keeps viewer and contributor access free on higher tiers
cons: Per-maker pricing can become expensive as more product managers and stakeholders need edit access
Business plan requires a minimum of two makers and Enterprise requires a minimum of five
AI credit-based system adds complexity to understanding total cost at scale
Full enterprise-grade features (SSO, SCIM, custom roles) are reserved for the highest, custom-priced tier

faqs.question: Is Productboard free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Productboard offers a Free plan with unlimited roadmaps, 500 feedback notes, 25 contributors, and 50 AI credits per month, with no credit card required.

faqs.question: How much does Productboard cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $19/maker/month for Plus (annual billing), $59/maker/month for Business (minimum two makers), and custom Enterprise pricing with a minimum of five makers.

faqs.question: When was Productboard founded?
faqs.answer: Productboard was founded in 2014 by Hubert Palan and Daniel Hejl.

faqs.question: Where is Productboard headquartered?
faqs.answer: Productboard is dual-headquartered in San Francisco, California, and Prague, Czech Republic, with additional offices in Vancouver, Dublin, London, and Brno.

faqs.question: What is Spark in Productboard?
faqs.answer: Spark is Productboard's AI feature set that helps summarize feedback, generate insights, and support prioritization decisions using a credit-based system.

faqs.question: How much funding has Productboard raised?
faqs.answer: Productboard has raised approximately $261.7 million in total funding across seven rounds, including a $125 million Series D in 2022.

faqs.question: Does Productboard offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, a 14-day free trial is available on the Business plan.

faqs.question: What is a 'maker' seat in Productboard pricing?
faqs.answer: A maker is a paid seat for someone actively creating or managing product plans; contributor and viewer access is included at no extra cost depending on the plan.

use_cases.title: Customer feedback aggregation
use_cases.description: Centralizing feedback from support, sales, and surveys into a single system tied to roadmap decisions.
use_cases.audience: Product managers and product ops teams

use_cases.title: Feature prioritization
use_cases.description: Scoring and ranking competing feature requests against customer impact and strategic goals.

use_cases.title: Roadmap communication
use_cases.description: Sharing internal and external-facing roadmaps to align engineering, sales, and customers.
